title: Viguiera multiflora
taxonomy: Viguiera multiflora is classified within the family Asteraceae. This classification places it in the order Asterales and the clade Eudicots. The plant is further grouped into the tribe Heliantheae and the subfamily Asteroideae. Its classification at the genus level places it within the genus Viguiera. At the species level, it is classified as Viguiera multiflora.
uses: Viguiera multiflora, also known as the Mexican Gold or Mexican Gold daisy, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Its roots and leaves are said to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which may help in treating wounds and infections. In some cultures, it is used to treat fever, rheumatism, and other conditions. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of its medicinal uses. Further studies are required to fully understand the potential benefits and risks of using Viguiera multiflora for medicinal purposes.

constituents: Viguiera multiflora is known to contain a variety of active constituents, including sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids. These compounds have been reported to have anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties. The sesquiterpene lactones present in Viguiera multiflora are thought to be responsible for its medicinal and therapeutic effects. parts: Viguiera multiflora, also known as the Mexican sunflower, is a plant that has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. intro: Viguiera multiflora, also known as "cascara sagrada's relative," is a flowering plant that belongs to the daisy family (Asteraceae). The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, providing relief from digestive issues such as constipation and diarrhea due to its anthraquinone content, which acts as a laxative and helps to regulate bowel movements. The bioactive constituents of Viguiera multiflora include anthraquinones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Viguiera multiflora is often used to make herbal tea, tinctures, and capsules, which are used to treat various gastrointestinal disorders.
